description: The direct evidential 'hi' explicitly claims that the speaker perceived the event through their own senses. An unmarked assertion names no source, so 'hi' is chosen when witnesshood itself matters. The inferential 'ke' (INFER) instead signals a conclusion drawn from signs.
articulatory_notes: Breath passes freely through the open glottis for 'h' and moves into high-front 'i'. Stress falls across the whole particle.
usage_notes: The Slot 1 sequence puts evidentiality after voice and before modality. One evidential particle is the limit for a clause, and past witnesshood keeps tense first in the sequence 'to hi'.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: In testimony or disagreement, 'hi' puts the speaker on record as a witness before the evidence is weighed.
